prompt: Using ONLY the provided SLA document, answer: In a 30-day billing month, a customer's service was unavailable for a total of 10 hours, of which 6 hours was scheduled maintenance announced to the customer 5 days in advance and the remaining 4 hours was an unplanned outage. (1) What is the Monthly Uptime Percentage, and which service-credit tier applies? (2) State every condition the customer must satisfy to actually receive that credit, and the form the credit takes. Cite the exact clause numbers you rely on. If the document does not determine something, say so explicitly rather than guessing.

inputs.name: hosting-sla.md
inputs.content:
# Managed Hosting Service Level Agreement (SLA v4)

Clause 1. Uptime Commitment. Provider commits to a Monthly Uptime Percentage of at least 99.9% for the Production tier.

Clause 2. Calculation. Monthly Uptime Percentage = (Total Minutes in the month - Downtime Minutes) / Total Minutes in the month, expressed as a percentage. For all calculations a 30-day month is treated as exactly 43,200 minutes.

Clause 3. Service Credit Tiers. If the Monthly Uptime Percentage falls below the commitment, the customer is eligible for a service credit calculated as a percentage of the monthly fee for the affected service:
  (a) below 99.9% but at or above 99.0%: 10% credit;
  (b) below 99.0% but at or above 95.0%: 25% credit;
  (c) below 95.0%: 50% credit.

Clause 4. Definition of Downtime. "Downtime" means minutes during which the Production service is unavailable, EXCLUDING: (i) scheduled maintenance that Provider announced to the customer at least 48 hours in advance; (ii) unavailability caused by the customer's own configuration or applications; and (iii) events of force majeure. Excluded minutes are not counted as Downtime Minutes in Clause 2.

Clause 5. Form of Credit. A service credit is applied as a discount against a FUTURE monthly invoice. It is not paid as a cash refund and has no cash value.

Clause 6. Claim Requirement. To receive any service credit the customer MUST submit a written claim to support within 30 days of the end of the affected month, including the dates, times, and Provider-generated incident logs for each outage. A claim submitted after the 30-day window is forfeited and no credit is owed.

Clause 7. No Stacking. Only a single tier applies in any given month: the customer receives the credit for the single highest applicable tier under Clause 3, not the sum of multiple tiers. The maximum service credit in any month is 50%.

Clause 8. Sole Remedy. Service credits under this SLA are the customer's sole and exclusive remedy for any failure to meet the Uptime Commitment.

grading.guidance: Correct answer: The 6 hours of scheduled maintenance is EXCLUDED from Downtime under Clause 4(i) because it was announced 5 days (well over 48 hours) in advance, so only the 4-hour unplanned outage counts = 240 Downtime Minutes. Monthly Uptime Percentage = (43,200 - 240) / 43,200 = 42,960 / 43,200 = 99.44% (accept 99.4%). That is below 99.9% but at or above 99.0%, so Clause 3(a) applies: a 10% service credit. Conditions to receive it (Clause 6): the customer must submit a written claim to support within 30 days of month-end, including dates, times, and Provider incident logs; a late claim is forfeited. Form (Clause 5): a discount on a future invoice, not a cash refund. Also note Clause 7 (only the single highest tier applies, no stacking). THE TRAP: counting all 10 hours as downtime (600 minutes) gives 98.61%, which would wrongly trigger the 25% tier under Clause 3(b). Penalize heavily any answer that counts the scheduled maintenance as downtime, reports 98.6% / 25% / 50%, treats the credit as a cash refund, or omits the mandatory 30-day claim requirement. A 10 gives 99.44% and 10% credit, correctly excludes the maintenance citing Clause 4(i), lists the Clause 6 claim conditions and Clause 5 future-invoice form, and cites the specific clauses. A 1 counts the maintenance and reports the 25% (or higher) tier.

calibrationAnchors.answer: Total downtime was 10 hours = 600 minutes, so uptime = (43200-600)/43200 = 98.61%. That is below 99.0%, so the customer gets a 25% service credit, which will be refunded to them automatically.

The 6 hours of scheduled maintenance is excluded from Downtime under Clause 4(i) because it was announced 5 days in advance (more than the 48-hour minimum), so only the 4-hour unplanned outage counts: 240 Downtime Minutes. Per Clause 2, Monthly Uptime Percentage = (43,200 - 240) / 43,200 = 99.44%. That is below 99.9% but at or above 99.0%, so Clause 3(a) gives a 10% service credit (not 25% - that would require counting the excluded maintenance). To receive it the customer must, per Clause 6, submit a written claim within 30 days of month-end with dates, times, and Provider incident logs, or the credit is forfeited. Per Clause 5 the credit is a discount on a future invoice, not a cash refund, and per Clause 7 only this single highest tier applies.
